I'd like to begin by being very clear about what school sport New Zealand is and what it isn't. School Sport New Zealand is an independent membership organization. It was established by schools, it's governed by schools and it exists for schools and their enrolled students. Our mandate is not universal youth participation.

Our mandate is school-based sport delivered as part of the secondary school education system under the authority and the responsibility of schools themselves. That distinction is critical to understanding our position on this position, uh, petition. We fully acknowledge that home education is a lawful and legitimate pathway in New Zealand.

We also acknowledge the sincerity of the concerns raised by the num, by the families represented by Hisa. And we recognize the personal impact of exclusion from sport that that can have on young people. This is precisely why school sport New Zealand has not taken a closed door approach over many years, but particularly since 2019.

School Sport New Zealand has actively engaged with non-membership organizations, including hisa, to explore how participation opportunities can be created within our school sport calendar without undermining the purpose and the integrity of the system that we're responsible for. That engagement has been real, it's been sustained and it's been tangible over several years.

Homeschool students have been able to and have participated in school sport New Zealand sanctioned events through agreed mechanisms. In total, approximately 26 homeschool students participated in sanctioned events in 2025 alongside more than 74,000 secondary school students. That's not because of limitation or because of issues around how they can access.

I would, I would argue that the exemption process is relatively simple and that we didn't deny any actual participation through those exemption processes. It just represents the scale of the participation and the scale of the challenge in front of us. This participation has primarily occurred through what we have as Cup and Carnival events and through local and regional discretion where organizers and schools have chosen to be inclusive where they can be.

The question is really about championship tier events and where the school sort sport New Zealand should be required to allow full inclusion at that level of our offerings. Championship events are designed differently. They're designed purposefully in that, in that regard, they exist to determine which school is the national champion.

They sit at a point where school identity, school accountability, competitive integrity, and educational purpose inters most strongly. When a school enters a championship event, it does so in its own name under the authority of its principal with the responsibility for student welfare eligibility and conduct. Those events are not simply competitions, they're an extension of the school environment.

For that reason, school sport new New Zealand members have consistently chosen to retain championship events as school only. That decision's not made lightly. It has been debated for decades and most recently through the largest review of eligibility regulations in more than 15 years, involving extensive commonwealth consultation with schools and sports across the country.

Finally, I want to correct an important misconception that appears throughout the petition school sport. New Zealand does not control all local and regional school sport competitions. Regional sport delivery is governed and managed in different ways across the country by regional sport trusts, by college sport organizations and local school groupings.
